The adoption of Enterprise AI has stalled 42% of large companies (over 1,000 employees), while 40% are stuck in exploration or experimentation phases and pilots are unable to translate into production – a gap that is important because early adopters are making progress, with 59% accelerating their AI investments and rollouts over the past 24 months.

The primary bottleneck is a shortage of skilled workers: 33% of companies cite limited AI know-how as a top obstacle, one out of five lack employees with the right skills and 16% cannot find qualified new recruits to fill the gap.
Data complexity blocks 25% of organizations, ethical concerns block 23%, integration difficulties and high cost of each block about 21–22%.
Geographical differences are strong: India (59%), the United Arab Emirates (58%), Singapore (53%), France (26%), Australia (29%), Spain (28%).
Financial services lead through industry, with about 50% use of AI, compared to 37% in telecommunications.
Generative AI introduces additional friction: 57% of non-adopters cite data protection and 43% cite trust and transparency as inhibitors.

Although 85% of IT professionals believe that consumers prefer companies with transparent AI practices, less than half have taken concrete steps – only 27% actively reduce bias, 37% pursue data excellence and 44% have developed ethical AI policies.

Cost reduction and process automation (42%) and easier access tools (45%) are the strongest adoption drivers, while the dominant applications are IT automation (33%), security and threat detection (26%) and AI governance (25%).
Only 34% of the organizations reskil employees to work alongside AI tools.

Organisations should prioritize the closure of the skills gap through structured reskilling programs and accessible, less complex AI-tooling, as the 33% barrier is the largest single inhibitor, only 34% actively train workers, and the data show that companies that have overcome the early barriers are now increasing their advantage through accelerated investment.
